behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure is primarily classification logic (rename vs add+delete) on change records. The behavior text does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior, AI/ML output, or multi-step agent flow. Rejected Integration/System because no concrete behavior text says the bug only appears at module handoff or with real git process output; this can be caught by feeding representative rename records/status lines into the classifier function.
behaviors.test_description: Test the local categorization function with fixture inputs representing rename events (including typical git rename status forms) and assert: `renamed += 1`, `added`/`deleted` are not incremented for the same file pair.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is about robustness against real git diff behavior for binary content. MOCK-HIDE risk is high: mocked/paraphrased diff text can miss real subprocess output shape/encoding and error paths, so tests may pass while real runs crash. No AI/ML dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary real git repo, commit baseline, add/modify a binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI as a subprocess against that repo, and assert process exits successfully and report includes the binary file in counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: Unborn-HEAD behavior is a real git state surfaced through subprocess exit codes/messages. In-process mocks can hide this exact failure mode. Rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly targets a repository state that is defined by real git infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real temporary git repository with zero commits, execute the CLI subprocess, and assert: exit code is success, stdout is a valid empty structured report, and no error text is emitted.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly anchors correctness to the repository’s actual history, which is defined by real git operations across commits/renames/deletions/modifications. Mocked history objects can diverge from real git semantics and hide failures. No LLM dependence or multi-step agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a real git repo with a known sequence of commits producing added/deleted/renamed/modified files, run CLI on it, and compare reported counts to expected counts derived from that concrete commit sequence.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real git index vs working-tree semantics, which are infrastructure-level states. Mocks frequently collapse these states and can miss real differences. Rejected Integration because the failure boundary is git’s real staging behavior, not just internal module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temporary git repo, create equivalent staged and unstaged change scenarios, run CLI subprocess in each scenario, normalize stable fields if needed, and assert outputs are identical.
